metadata.title: Agentic Research Course
metadata.author: Seyed Yahya Shirazi, Ph.D.
metadata.description: Week 4: CI/CD and Code Quality -- Automated Checks Outside the Repo

elements.content: # Agentic Research Course
Week 4: CI/CD and Code Quality

**Seyed Yahya Shirazi, Ph.D.**
Assistant Project Scientist, Swartz Center for Computational Neuroscience
UC San Diego

[Open Science Collective](https://osc.earth)
Course: [courses.osc.earth/agentic-research](https://courses.osc.earth/agentic-research/) | Discord: [discord.gg/3JtBrU3c](https://discord.gg/3JtBrU3c)

slides.speakerNotes:
- Welcome back

- Last week: epic + sub-issues + worktrees + plan + review (structure inside the repo)

- Today: CI/CD -- automated checks **outside** the repo, on every push

- Walkthrough format: ~28 min presentation, then we wire CI into the practicum live

- Course site and Discord on the slide

elements.content: ## Where We Are

items.text: **Week 1 -- Git and GitHub.** The safety net.
**Week 2 -- Claude Code.** The agent.
**Week 3 -- Project management.** Epic, sub-issues, worktrees, plan, review.
**Today -- CI/CD.** Automated checks on every push.

elements.content: Last week, checks you run. This week, checks the **machine** runs for you.

slides.speakerNotes:
[Press right 5x to reveal fragments]

- Recap quickly. Don't re-teach Week 3.

- The transition: last week your discipline carried the workflow. This week the machine takes over the boring parts.

elements.content: ## The Problem CI/CD Solves

elements.alt: Code is pushed to a runner that runs lint, test, build steps and reports pass or fail

items.text: **'Works on my machine'** -- the canonical research bug. Different OS, different Python, different toolbox.
Agent commits a change that breaks an unrelated module; nobody notices for two weeks.
Manual checking does not scale. Under deadline, humans skip it.
Research output is only as reproducible as the **pipeline** that produced it.

slides.speakerNotes:
[Press right 4x to reveal fragments]

- Tell the war story. People remember stories, not lists.

- The agent angle: AI commits a lot of code; the surface area for silent breakage grows fast.

- Land the last bullet hard. CI is reproducibility infrastructure, not just a code-quality tool.

elements.content: ## CI vs CD, Plainly

### Continuous Integration

Every push runs lint, type, tests.

Broken `main` becomes mechanically impossible.

### Continuous Deployment

Every passing build can ship.

Docker image, paper PDF, docs site, release tag.
For research, **CI** is the part you use every day. **CD** usually means publishing docs or tagging releases.

slides.speakerNotes:
- Two short definitions; do not overload.

- CI vs CD is one of the most googled and least useful distinctions in software. Explain in 60 seconds and move on.

- For research, the bulk of value is on the CI side. CD shows up when you publish docs or release a versioned tool.

elements.content: ## How GitHub Actions Works

elements.alt: Cycle of integrate, build, push, monitor with a center checkmark

items.text: A YAML file in `.github/workflows/` is read on every event.
GitHub spins up a **runner** (Ubuntu, macOS, or Windows VM).
Runner executes your **steps**: commands or **actions** from the Marketplace.
Logs and artifacts saved on the run.

elements.content: April 2026 defaults: `ubuntu-latest` = 24.04, `macos-latest` = macOS 15 (Apple Silicon), `windows-latest` = Server 2025.

slides.speakerNotes:
[Press right 5x to reveal fragments]

- Mental model: every push triggers a fresh VM somewhere in Microsoft's data center; that VM runs your YAML.

- Reusable actions = the npm/uv of CI. actions/checkout, actions/setup-python, etc.

- macos-latest flipped to Apple Silicon. If your code or any toolbox depends on x86_64, pin to macos-13 explicitly. This is the most common 2025 footgun.

elements.content: ## YAML Anatomy -- Five Parts to Know

elements.alt: An annotated CI workflow file with arrows pointing at name, on, jobs, runs-on, and steps

items.text: **name** -- what shows up in the GitHub UI.
**on** -- which events fire the workflow (push, PR, schedule, manual).
**jobs** -- one or more parallel jobs; each gets its own runner.
**runs-on** -- which OS the job runs on.
**steps** -- the commands or actions, top to bottom.

elements.content: YAML indentation is significant. **Spaces only**, never tabs. A misplaced two-space indent is the #1 reason new workflows fail.

slides.speakerNotes:
[Press right 6x to reveal fragments]

- Most informative slide of the deck. Read each label aloud.

- Pause on `on:` -- this is the single most-asked question from new users (the next slide).

- Tabs vs spaces: real world bug. Mention how the GitHub UI's web editor highlights it but a copy-paste from a blog rarely does.

elements.content: ## Triggers -- Controlling When CI Fires

elements.alt: Five trigger icons: push, pull_request, schedule, workflow_dispatch, paths filter

elements.content: `paths:` filters skip a 10-minute build when only the README changed. Use it on every workflow that touches code. Full YAML examples in the speaker notes.

slides.speakerNotes:
- Five trigger types is enough for 95% of research workflows.

- Reference YAML to read aloud:

  on:
    push:
      branches: [main]
      paths: ['src/**', 'pyproject.toml']
    pull_request:
    schedule:
      - cron: '0 6 * * *'   # 06:00 UTC daily
    workflow_dispatch:

- Land the paths filter. Researchers iterate on README and notebook outputs constantly; you don't want to burn CI minutes on every doc commit.

- workflow_dispatch is the manual run button -- handy for re-running the nightly job ad hoc.

elements.content: ## Tools Matter, Especially in CI

items.text: Faster feedback = more iterations per session.
Single-binary tools = fewer moving parts in CI.
Lockfiles = bit-identical envs across collaborators and CI.

elements.content: Both chains work. Pick one. The next four slides show **why we pick the modern one**.

slides.speakerNotes:
[Press right 4x to reveal fragments]

- Transition slide; do not dwell.

- Land the callout: head off the 'but I use pip' objection before slide 9.

- The whole tooling block is a justification, not a sales pitch.

elements.content: ## Python: UV Replaces pip, conda, venv

elements.alt: Bar chart: pip 21.4s vs uv 2.6s on cold install of JupyterLab

items.text: **Locked deps** -- `uv.lock` pins every transitive package.
**Fast** -- ~8x cold install; up to ~100x with warm cache.
**One binary** -- replaces pip, venv, pyenv, pip-tools.
**Tools** -- `uv tool install ruff` puts CLIs on your PATH.

elements.content: Lockfiles solve **"but it ran on my laptop in 2024."** Same `uv.lock` -> same packages -> same numbers.

slides.speakerNotes:
[Press right 5x to reveal fragments]

- UV 0.11.x as of April 2026; release cadence is roughly weekly.

- The 21.4s vs 2.6s number is from the JupyterLab benchmark on cold install. Cite if asked.

- Researcher translation: this is the slide where the audience feels the value. Land it. Two years from now your reviewer asks you to re-run the analysis; with uv.lock you can.

elements.content: ## Python: ruff + ty Replace Black, Flake8, isort, mypy

items.text: **ruff** -- lint **and** format in one Rust binary; ~10-100x faster than flake8/black.
**ty** -- Astral's type checker; mypy-compatible logic, much faster.
One config block, sub-second feedback, identical behavior locally and in CI.
`ruff check --fix --unsafe-fixes` autofixes most lint issues.

elements.code:
# pyproject.toml
[tool.ruff]
line-length = 100
target-version = "py312"
[tool.ruff.lint]
select = ["E", "F", "I", "UP", "B"]
fix = true
[tool.ty]
respect-ignore-files = true

elements.content: ty is still **beta** (April 2026, v0.0.x). Stable for daily research use; expect minor API churn before 1.0.

slides.speakerNotes:
[Press right 5x to reveal fragments]

- ruff replaces four tools at once. That's not marketing; you can delete black, flake8, isort, and pyupgrade from your dev deps the day you adopt ruff.

- ty is still 0.0.x (April 2026). Be honest about it. The breakage so far has been minor; if you want a 1.0-stable type checker today, mypy is fine.

- The pyproject snippet on the left is real -- copyable.

elements.content: ## JS/TS: Bun + Biome

items.text: **Bun** -- package manager + runtime + test runner. Replaces npm, node, jest.
**Biome** -- lint + format for JS, TS, JSX, JSON, CSS, GraphQL. Community fork of Rome.
97% Prettier-compatible formatting; 450+ rules from ESLint and typescript-eslint.

elements.content: Bun 1.3.x and Biome 2.4.x are production-ready in April 2026. JS/TS research tooling has caught up with the Rust-on-Python wave.

slides.speakerNotes:
[Press right 5x to reveal fragments]

- Some researchers will say 'I don't write JS.' Most do, indirectly: Jupyter extensions, web visualizations, doc tooling.

- The story is identical to Python: one binary replaces the pile.

- Biome 2.0 GA shipped in 2025; we are on 2.4.x as of April 2026.

elements.content: ## Spell-Check: typos vs cspell

items.text: Both check code, comments, and docs.
**typos** wins on speed and simplicity for small-to-medium repos.
**cspell** wins when you need rich per-language dictionaries (medical, legal, multilingual).
Either is fine. The point is **add a typo job to CI** so your README stops drifting.

slides.speakerNotes:
[Press right 4x to reveal fragments]

- This slide ends the cspell-vs-typos debate in 30 seconds.

- Course companion repo uses `typos` (added in PR #9). Live demo can show that workflow file.

- The actionable: add a typo job; pick whichever; move on.

elements.content: ## The Core Message

elements.alt: Two stacks of tools side by side: classical chain on the left with 9 boxes, modern chain on the right with 3 grouped boxes

elements.content: Pick **one** chain per language. Pin it in `pyproject.toml` / `package.json`. Let CI enforce it. Mixed chains double the surface area for drift.

slides.speakerNotes:
- Philosophical anchor of the tooling block.

- Land it with conviction so slides 14-15 (pre-commit + pipeline shape) feel like execution, not opinion.

- 'Mixed chains double the surface area for drift' -- say this. It's the line that lets people give up on their old toolchain without ego loss.

elements.content: ## Pre-Commit Hooks -- Catch Issues in 200 ms

elements.code:
# .pre-commit-config.yaml
repos:
  - repo: https://github.com/astral-sh/ruff-pre-commit
    rev: v0.15.12
    hooks:
      - id: ruff-check
        args: [--fix]
      - id: ruff-format
  - repo: https://github.com/crate-ci/typos
    rev: v1.45.2
    hooks:
      - id: typos

items.text: Runs on **staged files only** before each commit.
Same tools as CI (`ruff`, `biome`, `typos`); ~600x faster than waiting for the runner.
Auto-fixes touch the file -- re-`git add` and re-commit.

elements.content: Same tools, **staged files only**. `uv tool install pre-commit && pre-commit install` -- one line, lifetime of guardrails.

slides.speakerNotes:
- Pre-commit hooks run the linters on staged files only. 200 ms locally vs 2 min on the CI runner.

- The two install lines work on Mac, Linux, Windows-WSL.

- Failure mode: a hook auto-fixes the file. The commit fails because the working tree changed. Run `git add` again and re-commit. This trips up first-timers; warn them.

elements.content: ## Pipeline Shape -- Lint, Type, Test, Coverage

elements.alt: Horizontal pipeline: install, lint, type, test, coverage with green check at the end

items.text: Each stage is a check; failure stops the run and blocks the PR.
Coverage report uploaded as a build artifact -- readable from the PR.
Same shape for Python, JS/TS, R, Julia. Tools change; topology does not.

slides.speakerNotes:
[Press right 3x to reveal fragments]

- Standard pipeline shape. Five stages cover 95% of research projects.

- Coverage as artifact: HTML report or codecov badge in the PR.

- The topology lesson: when you switch languages, you keep the shape and swap the tools.

elements.content: ## Codecov -- Coverage Tracking + PR Comments

elements.code:
# .github/workflows/ci.yml
- run: uv run pytest --cov --cov-report=xml

- uses: codecov/codecov-action@v5
  with:
    files: ./coverage.xml
    token: ${{ secrets.CODECOV_TOKEN }}

items.text: **Badge** in your README so reviewers see coverage at a glance.
**PR comment** shows coverage delta -- did this PR raise or lower it?
**Trend graph** tracks coverage over time across releases.
Free for public repos; works with `pytest --cov`, `bun test --coverage`, MATLAB Cobertura output.

elements.content: Don't chase 100%. 70-80% on the analytical core is plenty; aim for **direction** (delta per PR), not absolute number.

slides.speakerNotes:
[Press right 5x to reveal fragments]

- Codecov is the most-used coverage host; Coveralls is a fine alternative.

- The PR comment is where reviewers actually see coverage -- the badge is for marketing.

- 70-80% target: analytical pipelines have a lot of plumbing code (CLI parsing, file IO) that does not benefit from heavy testing. Cover the math, the parsers, and the failure modes; skip the trivial.

- For matlab-mcp projects: MATLAB's MOTest framework can emit Cobertura XML which Codecov accepts.

elements.content: ## matlab-actions/setup-matlab

items.text: Official action, current `v3.0.1` (April 2026); MATLAB R2021a+ on Linux, macOS, Windows.
Companions: `run-tests`, `run-command`, `run-build`.
**License-free in CI for public repos.** Private repos use a batch licensing token.

elements.code:
jobs:
  matlab-tests:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: matlab-actions/setup-matlab@v3
        with:
          release: R2024b
          products: >
            Signal_Processing_Toolbox
            Statistics_and_Machine_Learning_Toolbox
      - uses: matlab-actions/run-tests@v3

elements.content: First time MATLAB joins the same CI pipeline as your Python. Live runs on every push, no manual license server.

slides.speakerNotes:
[Press right 5x to reveal fragments]

- `matlab-actions/setup-matlab` (MathWorks-maintained). Current major v3, latest tag v3.0.1 as of April 2026.

- License-free CI: the action automatically licenses MATLAB and most toolboxes for public repos. Private repos need a batch licensing token (MLM_LICENSE_TOKEN secret).

- This is huge for reproducibility. Your shot-change analysis can run on every push the same way pytest does.

elements.content: ## Limits of MATLAB in CI

elements.alt: Hosted runner with MATLAB working but Python engine blocked, with a self-hosted runner showing all features working

elements.content: **MATLAB Engine for Python is blocked under license-free CI.** Direct quote from the official `setup-matlab` README: "Public project and MATLAB batch licensing do not support external language interfaces, including MATLAB Engine APIs for Python." This is **the** matlab-mcp pain point.

slides.speakerNotes:
- The boomerang for matlab-mcp users. Be honest about it.

- Direct quote is from the official setup-matlab README. Cite verbatim if asked.

- Other limits worth mentioning briefly: not every MathWorks toolbox is licensed in CI mode (transformation products like MATLAB Coder and Compiler are excluded); Apple Silicon support depends on MATLAB release (R2023b+ recommended; consult MathWorks support page for the exact matrix).

- Do not dwell. Move to the workaround on the next slide.

elements.content: ## Strategy for matlab-mcp Projects

items.text: **Hosted runner** -- run Python wrapper code: lint, type check, unit tests. Fast, free, every push.
**Self-hosted runner** -- your Mac or lab workstation, registered to GitHub. Run the MATLAB analysis here with your full license.
**Or:** trigger the MATLAB job manually with `workflow_dispatch` instead of every push.
Don't gate the cheap stuff on the expensive MATLAB job. Split the pipeline.

elements.content: Practical for the HBN practicum: Python wrappers + event-table validators run on hosted runners every PR. The full EEGLAB pipeline runs on a self-hosted Mac when triggered.

slides.speakerNotes:
[Press right 5x to reveal fragments]

- Self-hosted runners are free to set up. GitHub provides the registration script; you run it on your Mac or workstation.

- Security note: only register self-hosted runners on private repos. Public repos = anyone-can-fork = anyone-can-run-code-on-your-machine.

- Connect to the practicum: Phase 1-4 wrappers are pure Python; gates them on hosted runners. Phase 5-6 (ERSP precompute, group stats) run on the self-hosted Mac.

elements.content: ## Security Audits in CI

elements.alt: Shield icon over a lock symbol with three green check marks, listing pip-audit, bun audit, gitleaks below

items.text: **Dependencies** -- `uv run pip-audit` (Python), `bun audit` (JS).
**Secrets** -- GitHub free secret scanning + a pre-commit hook (`gitleaks`).
**Supply chain** -- pin actions by SHA, not just `@v4` -- prevents tag hijacks.
Run on every PR. Block merge on critical findings.

elements.content: Use sparingly. A noisy security scanner becomes the boy who cried wolf. Configure thresholds; review findings; do not just bury them.

slides.speakerNotes:
[Press right 5x to reveal fragments]

- Default GitHub secret scanning catches the well-known patterns (AWS keys, GitHub tokens). Add gitleaks for academic API keys.

- Pin-by-SHA: paranoid but cheap. Dependabot can update SHAs automatically. The 2024 tj-actions/changed-files compromise affected ~23k repos that were tag-pinned -- SHA pinning would have prevented it.

- Critical-only thresholds keep the noise down.

elements.content: ## `/project:setup-ci` -- One Command

items.text: Detects your repo's languages and writes the right templates.
Drops `.github/workflows/`, `.pre-commit-config.yaml`, language config.
You commit. Push. Pipeline turns green on the next run.

elements.alt: Folder tree showing files generated by setup-ci: github workflows, pre-commit config, pyproject.toml, biome.json

elements.content: Same family as `/project:init-project` from Week 3. Init scaffolds **inside** the repo; setup-ci scaffolds the **remote referee**.

slides.speakerNotes:
[Press right 5x to reveal fragments]

- `/project:setup-ci` is in the research-skills plugin (course companion).

- Scaffold first; customize second. Most researchers leave 90% of the generated config and tweak one or two thresholds.

- Connect to Week 3: init-project builds the inside, setup-ci builds the outside. Same architecture, different scope.

elements.content: ## Live Walkthrough Roadmap

items.text: Open the practicum repo from Week 3.
Run `/project:setup-ci` and annotate the produced YAML live.
Push and watch the pipeline run on GitHub.
Introduce a deliberate typo and type error -- watch CI catch both.

elements.code:
cd ~/agentic-research-practicum
claude
> /project:setup-ci

git add .github .pre-commit-config.yaml
git commit -m "add CI"
git push

gh run watch     # streams the run in your terminal

elements.content: Goal of the walkthrough: see CI **catch** something. A failing run with a clear error is worth more than ten passing runs.

slides.speakerNotes:
[Press right 6x to reveal fragments]

- The walkthrough starts the moment this slide goes up.

- Deliberate failure is the teaching moment. A red CI run is the most reassuring thing for a first-time CI user; it proves the system actually works.

- gh run watch streams the run in your terminal -- nicer than alt-tabbing to the browser.

elements.content: ## What Today Gives You / What's Next

### Today

- Every push linted, typed, tested, scanned
- Green or red badge on every PR
- The agent cannot ship breakage

### Week 5

- Literature search with `opencite`
- First **pure-research** week
- DOI -> BibTeX -> PDF -> markdown
- Foundation for grant + manuscript weeks
Before next week: `/project:setup-ci` on your own project. Push a broken commit. Watch CI catch it.

slides.speakerNotes:
- Frame today as infrastructure for the next six weeks.

- Week 5 is the first pure-research week. From here on, the engineering scaffolding fades into the background and the deliverables get more academic (lit reviews, grants, manuscripts, figures).

- The before-next-week ask is small and concrete.

elements.content: ## What We Have / What We Do Next

### Have

- Week 3 practicum + epic + Phase 1 PR
- Six phase sub-issues open

### Do Next, Live

1. `/project:setup-ci` on the practicum
2. Annotate the YAML
3. Push -> watch -> add a typo
4. Phase 1 PR with green CI
**Questions? Ask while CI runs.**

slides.speakerNotes:
- Final slide. Stays on screen until the agent starts typing.

- The bottom line invites questions during the live build, which is the highest-engagement window of the session.
